message.content: Adversaries may embed payloads within benign files to conceal malicious content, bypassing defenses and subverting trust controls without affecting digital signatures or notarization. This technique involves hiding payloads in various file formats, similar to but distinct from steganography. Common methods include embedding payloads within binaries, nesting executables within files of the same format, and using embedded content for process injection. These payloads can support malware modules, such as encrypting command-and-control (C2) communications. Notable examples include ComRAT's XOR encrypted communications module, Dtrack's encrypted payload dropper, and Invoke-PSImage's image file embedding. The potential impact includes undetected malware execution, compromised system processes, and secure C2 communications for adversaries. Mitigation strategies involve rigorous file integrity monitoring, advanced threat detection systems, and regular audits of digital signatures and notarization tickets to identify anomalies.
